BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: As a consequence of new snow and warming a very precarious avalanche situation will prevail. The conditions are unfavourable for backcountry touring outside marked and open pistes.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Above approximately 700 m snow will fall until late morning. As a consequence of new snow and a moderate wind, further wind slabs will form. They can be released easily and reach very large size. In many cases avalanches can be triggered in the various wind slab layers. This applies in particular on steep slopes above the tree line, as well as in gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain.
As a consequence of warming and solar radiation, the natural avalanche activity will rapidly increase, in particular in case of releases originating from steep sunny starting zones that still retain some snow.
Backcountry touring calls for great caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Up to 30 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall until late morning above approximately 1500 m. As a consequence of the moderate wind, fresh snow drift accumulations will form. Over a wide area new snow and wind slabs are lying on soft layers.
Sunshine and high temperatures will give rise from early morning to rapid and thorough wetting of the snowpack in particular on steep sunny slopes at low and intermediate altitudes.
Above approximately 1000 m a lot of snow is lying.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather conditions will give rise to gradual settling of the snowpack.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
A lot of new snow. As a consequence of new snow and wind the prevalence and size of the avalanche prone locations will increase. The snowpack will be in most cases unstable. Natural avalanches are possible over a wide area. Avalanches can penetrate deep layers reach very large size. As a consequence of the precipitation moist avalanches are possible.
Without question, it is inadvisable to engage in backcountry touring.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Somewhat older wind slabs remain for the foreseeable future prone to triggering. As a consequence of new snow and wind, further wind slabs will form.
Numerous weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on shady slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Considerable avalanche danger will prevail. Wind slabs and wet snow represent the main danger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Some snow will fall until late morning. As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate wind, sometimes large wind slabs will form. Wind slabs can be released, even by a single winter sport participant. This applies in particular on steep slopes, as well as in gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain also at intermediate altitudes.
Avalanches can be released in deeper layers in particular on steep shady slopes.
Medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large natural moist avalanches are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation, in particular in case of releases originating from steep sunny starting zones that have retained the snow thus far.
The avalanche prone locations are sometimes covered with new snow difficult to recognise. Careful route selection and spacing between individuals are recommended.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Over a wide area 10 to 20 cm of snow will fall until late morning. As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate wind from westerly directions, further wind slabs will form in particular above the tree line. The new snow and wind slabs are lying on soft layers on wind-protected shady slopes.

Intermediate and high altitudes: Isolated avalanche prone weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular on very steep shady slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The snow sport conditions outside marked and open pistes are precarious. The avalanche danger is within the uppermost range of danger level 3 (considerable).
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Avalanches can in many places be released in the old snowpack. In isolated cases these are large and can be released easily even by a single winter sport participant. As a consequence of warming, the likelihood of avalanches being released will increase. Caution is to be exercised in particular on west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 1800 m. Remotely triggered avalanches are probable. Individual natural avalanches are still not ruled out.

The avalanche prone locations are prevalent and are barely recognisable. Whumpfing sounds and the formation of shooting cracks when stepping on the snowpack are a clear indication of a weakly bonded snowpack.

Mostly small loose snow avalanches are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation. Individual gliding avalanches can also occur.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: In some localities up to 50 cm of snow has fallen since Tuesday. Some snow will fall. The new snow is lying on top of a weakly bonded old snowpack in particular on west, north and east facing slopes above approximately 1800 m. Distinct weak layers exist in the old snowpack.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Avalanches can as before be released, even by a single winter sport participant. The snowpack remains prone to triggering.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The avalanche prone locations are to be found especially on steep shady slopes above the tree line. Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
In some regions in some localities up to 5 cm of snow will fall on Friday.
Avalanches can be released in near-ground layers by small loads. Remotely triggered and natural avalanches are possible.
In addition the fresh and older wind slabs must be taken into account. Over a wide area new snow and wind slabs are lying on surface hoar.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The new snow of last week is lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack on shady slopes above the tree line. Towards its base, the snowpack is faceted and weak. Avalanches can be released in deeper layers very easily.
In addition the large surface-area wind slabs adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls are capable of being triggered.

BulletinMeasurements.highlights: The conditions are precarious for backcountry touring and other off-piste activities.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The avalanche danger is within the uppermost range of danger level 3 (considerable). Natural avalanches and loose snow slides are still possible.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The fresh snow of the last few days as well as the deep wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack. In particular on wind-loaded slopes avalanches can be released easily and reach medium size. Whumpfing sounds and the formation of shooting cracks when stepping on the snowpack and natural avalanches serve as an alarm indicating the danger. Remotely triggered avalanches are possible.
Distinct weak layers in the lower part of the snowpack can be released even now easily by individual winter sport participants in particular on west, north and east facing slopes. This applies in particular above the tree line on steep west, north and east facing slopes. Avalanches can release the weakly bonded old snow as well and reach medium size. The avalanche prone locations are covered with new snow and are therefore barely recognisable for beginners. They are numerous. The prevalence of avalanche prone locations and likelihood of triggering will increase with altitude. Caution is to be exercised in particular on steep west, north and east facing slopes above the tree line.
Caution and restraint are recommended.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The new snow and wind slabs of the last few days are poorly bonded with the old snowpack above the tree line.
Distinct weak layers exist in the old snowpack. This applies especially on west, north and east facing slopes.
Night: Over a wide area 5 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, has fallen above approximately 1200 m.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: On the border ridges towards France and Switzerland and as you climb higher the danger points are more widespread.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of new snow and a moderate wind from variable directions, avalanche prone wind slabs will form in the late morning in particular in the regions exposed to precipitation. The danger of dry avalanches will already increase in the late morning.
Single winter sport participants can release avalanches. Mostly they are shallow, caution is to be exercised in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls, and on very steep slopes in particular above the tree line. In some cases the wind slabs have bonded poorly with the old snowpack. The avalanche prone locations are barely recognisable.
On very steep shady slopes the avalanches can be triggered in deep layers of the snowpack. Slopes that have been little used this winter thus far are especially unfavourable.
Careful route selection and spacing between individuals are recommended.
As a consequence of warming, the natural avalanche activity will increase, in particular in case of releases originating from very steep starting zones.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Friday: 15 to 25 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, will fall above approximately 1400 m.
Wednesday: 5 to 10 cm of snow, but less in some localities, has fallen above approximately 1500 m.
Tuesday: 5 to 15 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, has fallen above approximately 2000 m.
The fresh snow and especially the wind-blown snow drifts, both soft and hard, cover a thin layer of old snow and partly also surface hoar.
Faceted weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on west, north and east facing slopes.
Since Wednesday on very steep slopes medium-sized and, in isolated cases, large avalanches occurred naturally, in particular avalanches with origins in high-altitude starting zones.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather conditions will facilitate a slow strengthening of the snowpack. The danger will not decrease for the time being.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: At intermediate and high altitudes a considerable avalanche danger will prevail.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Some snow will fall until late morning. As a consequence of new snow and a moderate wind from northwesterly directions, sometimes large wind slabs will form. This applies in particular on steep slopes, as well as in gullies and bowls, and behind abrupt changes in the terrain above the tree line. The fresh wind slabs can be released by small loads. Avalanches can be released in deeper layers in particular on very steep shady slopes.

Medium-sized to large natural dry avalanches are possible as a consequence of warming during the day and solar radiation. This applies in particular in case of releases originating from very steep sunny starting zones that have retained the snow thus far.

Careful route selection and spacing between individuals are recommended.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Over a wide area 5 to 15 cm of snow will fall until late morning. As a consequence of new snow and a sometimes moderate wind, further wind slabs will form. The new snow and wind slabs are lying on soft layers in particular on wind-protected shady slopes.

Intermediate and high altitudes: Isolated avalanche prone weak layers exist in the bottom section of the snowpack in particular on very steep shady slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: A treacherous avalanche situation will prevail. Wind slabs and weakly bonded old snow require caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Distinct weak layers in the lower part of the snowpack can be released easily by individual winter sport participants. In addition the fresh wind slabs are very easily triggered. Avalanches can release the weakly bonded old snow as well and reach medium size. Caution is to be exercised in particular on steep west, north and east facing slopes above the tree line.

Whumpfing sounds and the formation of shooting cracks when stepping on the snowpack and natural avalanches serve as an alarm indicating the danger. The avalanche prone locations are sometimes covered with new snow and are therefore difficult to recognise. Remotely triggered avalanches are possible. Individual natural avalanches are not entirely ruled out.

Caution and restraint are recommended.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
In some regions 15 to 25 cm of snow has fallen since Tuesday. This applies in particular on the Main Alpine Ridge and to the south. Up to 10 cm of snow will fall.
The new snow and wind slabs will be deposited on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack. Distinct weak layers exist in the old snowpack. This applies especially on west, north and east facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.highlights: The new snow represents the main danger.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: The new snow must be evaluated with care and prudence in all aspects.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: Over a wide area over a wide area 20 to 40 cm of snow has fallen above approximately 1500 m. The new snow can be released easily or naturally in all aspects. In particular in the regions exposed to heavier precipitation medium-sized dry avalanches are to be expected as a consequence of warming. The off-piste conditions are dangerous.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The new snow is lying on surface hoar above the tree line. Snow profiles and stability tests have confirmed the unfavourable bonding of the snowpack. Weak layers deep in the old snowpack are difficult to recognise. Towards its base, the snowpack is faceted and weak. Avalanches can be released in deeper layers very easily. Individual gliding avalanches can also be released in the night or in the morning.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: A treacherous avalanche situation will prevail. Wind slabs and weakly bonded old snow require caution.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Distinct weak layers in the lower part of the snowpack can be released easily by individual winter sport participants. In addition the fresh wind slabs are very easily triggered. Avalanches can release the weakly bonded old snow as well and reach medium size.
Caution is to be exercised in particular on steep west, north and east facing slopes above the tree line.

Whumpfing sounds and the formation of shooting cracks when stepping on the snowpack and natural avalanches serve as an alarm indicating the danger. The avalanche prone locations are sometimes covered with new snow and are therefore difficult to recognise. Remotely triggered avalanches are possible.
Caution and restraint are recommended.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
In some regions 15 to 25 cm of snow has fallen since Tuesday. Some snow will fall.
The new snow and wind slabs are lying on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack. Distinct weak layers exist in the old snowpack. This applies especially on west, north and east facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Moist slab avalanches are possible from the morning. In addition on southeast, south and west facing slopes, some small and medium-sized slab avalanches are possible. Areas where the snow cover is rather shallow where weaknesses exist in the old snowpack are especially unfavourable.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment: As the wind picks up, the likelihood of moist slab avalanches being released will increase quickly in particular on very steep slopes above approximately 1900 m. The surface of the snowpack will cool hardly at all during the overcast night and will already soften in the late morning. On very steep northwest, north and east facing slopes and above approximately 1900 m individual small and, in isolated cases, medium-sized moist slab avalanches are possible as a consequence of the strong westerly wind.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: The old snowpack consists of rounded crystals. The weather conditions gave rise to significant softening of the snowpack in all aspects below approximately 1800 m. These conditions will cause a very rapid rise in the danger of moist avalanches in particular above approximately 1800 m.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Near the 3-considerable danger zones and as you climb higher in altitude, danger spots are more widespread.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
As a consequence of new snow and a moderate wind from variable directions, mostly shallow wind slabs will form in the late morning in particular adjacent to ridgelines. The danger of dry avalanches will increase but remain within the current danger level.
Single winter sport participants can release avalanches, including medium-sized ones. Mostly they are shallow, caution is to be exercised in particular adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls, and on very steep slopes in particular above the tree line. The avalanche prone locations are barely recognisable.
On very steep shady slopes the avalanches can be released in deep layers of the snowpack and reach quite a large size. Slopes that have been little used this winter thus far are especially unfavourable.
Maintaining distances between individuals is recommended.
As a consequence of warming, the natural avalanche activity will slowly increase, in particular in case of releases originating from very steep starting zones.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Friday: 10 to 15 cm of snow, but less in some localities, will fall above approximately 1600 m.
Wednesday: 5 to 10 cm of snow, but less in some localities, has fallen above approximately 1500 m.
Tuesday: 5 to 15 cm of snow, and even more in some localities, has fallen above approximately 2000 m.
The fresh snow and especially the wind-blown snow drifts, both soft and hard, cover a thin layer of old snow and partly also surface hoar.
Faceted weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on west, north and east facing slopes.
Since Wednesday on very steep slopes small and medium-sized avalanches occurred naturally, in particular avalanches with origins in high-altitude starting zones.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: The weather conditions will facilitate a slow strengthening of the snowpack. The danger will persist.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint. Gliding avalanches can also occur.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
In some regions in some localities up to 10 cm of snow will fall on Friday.
Weak layers in the old snowpack necessitate caution and restraint.
Avalanches can be released by small loads.
Gliding avalanches can also occur at any time.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
Areas with glide cracks are to be avoided as far as possible.
Precarious weak layers exist in the old snowpack in particular on steep northwest, north and northeast facing slopes.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
The fresh wind slabs can be released easily in some cases. This applies especially on steep shady slopes, as well as adjacent to ridgelines and in gullies and bowls above the tree line. In isolated cases avalanches are medium-sized. The avalanche prone locations are sometimes covered with new snow and are therefore difficult to recognise.

Apart from the danger of being buried, restraint should be exercised in particular in view of the danger of avalanches sweeping people along and giving rise to falls.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment:
The fresh wind slabs will be deposited on the unfavourable surface of an old snowpack at elevated altitudes. The old snowpack consists of faceted crystals.

The snowpack will be generally subject to considerable local variations.
BulletinMeasurements.tendencyComment: Moderate avalanche danger will prevail. Fresh wind slabs require caution.

BulletinMeasurements.avActivityHighlights: In very isolated cases the avalanches in these regions are small and can be released by large loads.
BulletinMeasurements.avActivityComment:
Mostly the avalanches are small and can only be released by large loads.
Gliding avalanches can also occur.
BulletinMeasurements.snowpackStructureComment: Areas with glide cracks are to be avoided. Below approximately 1000 m a little snow is lying.
